dependencies: Sole-sourced products from certain manufacturing sites
Complex manufacturing processes requiring regulatory approvals
Reliance on third-party contract manufacturing organizations (CMOs)
Certain third-party suppliers are sole or exclusive source of materials
Dependence on logistical service providers for global supply chain
risks: Manufacturing problems and capacity imbalances may cause product launch delays, inventory shortages, recalls or unanticipated costs
Minor deviations in manufacturing processes could result in delays, inventory shortages, product recalls, regulatory action
Production interruptions from equipment malfunctions, material shortages, labor problems, natural disasters, power outages, criminal activities
Outbreak of highly contagious diseases near production sites could halt production
Failure of CMOs to meet obligations could impact ability to meet demand
Delays in receiving regulatory approvals for manufacturing changes
Supply chain disruptions from COVID-19 pandemic impacted ability to meet customer demand
Increased labor and material costs due to supply constraints, weather conditions, government regulations
Potential disruptions from climate change impacts on manufacturing processes

regulatory_risks: Subject to various federal, state, local and foreign environmental, health and safety laws and regulations
Failure to comply with regulatory requirements could result in fines, penalties, product withdrawals, facility shutdowns, civil or criminal prosecution
Regulatory agencies can modify or revoke permits and authorizations
Changes in Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) requirements
Delays in regulatory approvals including from prolonged U.S. government shutdowns
Products subject to re-review and may lose approvals after reaching market
Restrictions and bans on use of antibacterials in food-producing animals
New regulations in Italy requiring electronic prescriptions for antibiotics
Increased regulation of livestock industry operations
Environmental regulations including PFAS restrictions in EU and U.S.
Climate-related disclosure requirements (EU CSRD, California climate laws)
Chemical regulation changes (REACH in EU, EPA regulations in U.S.)
Foreign trade controls administered by U.S. agencies (BIS, CBP, OFAC)
Compliance with FCPA and foreign anti-corruption laws
Data privacy regulations (GDPR in EU, state privacy laws in U.S., China PIPL, India DPDP Act)
Sanctions regulations affecting business in Russia and Iran
Potential changes to international trade agreements and tariffs
Regulatory requirements for animal testing subject to increased restrictions
FDA and USDA regulatory oversight of products and manufacturing facilities
EMA centralized approval process in EU
MARA regulatory requirements in China becoming increasingly stringent
Pillar Two global minimum tax provisions effective 2024

sanction_risks: OFAC sanctions affecting business with Iran - company sells limited humanitarian animal health products in compliance with sanctions
Voluntary disclosure to OFAC in 2020 regarding Platinum Performance transactions potentially violating Iran sanctions (resolved with No Action letter in 2023)
Sanctions affecting business with Russia - company sells limited humanitarian products in compliance
Risk of being subject to sanctions for violations of export controls or trade restrictions
Potential penalties including fines, denial of export privileges, asset seizures, debarment from government contracts
